title: ब्रह्मा अष्टकम्
devanagariText:
॥ श्री ब्रह्मा अष्टकम् ॥

चतुर्वक्त्रं चतुर्बाहुं सृष्टिस्थित्यन्तकारणम्।
अक्षमालाधरं देवं ब्रह्माणं प्रणमाम्यहम्॥ १॥

कमलासनमासीनं श्वेतवर्णं चतुर्मुखम्।
वेदवाचं विधातारं ब्रह्माणं प्रणमाम्यहम्॥ २॥

कमण्डलुधरं हस्ते स्रुक्स्रुवौ च कराम्बुजे।
पद्मासने स्थितं नित्यं ब्रह्माणं प्रणमाम्यहम्॥ ३॥

हंसवाहनमारूढं मेखलायज्ञसूत्रिणम्।
हिरण्यगर्भं वरदं ब्रह्माणं प्रणमाम्यहम्॥ ४॥

सावित्रीसहितं देवं वेदमातृसमन्वितम्।
प्रजापतिं जगत्स्रष्टा ब्रह्माणं प्रणमाम्यहम्॥ ५॥

राजसं गुणसम्पन्नं सृष्टिकर्तारमीश्वरम्।
विरिञ्चिं पितरं विश्वस्य ब्रह्माणं प्रणमाम्यहम्॥ ६॥

चतुर्वेदस्वरूपं च चतुर्युगविधायकम्।
आदिकवि कविं वन्दे ब्रह्माणं प्रणमाम्यहम्॥ ७॥

नाभिकमलसम्भूतं विष्णोर्नारायणस्य च।
शतानन्दं सदानन्दं ब्रह्माणं प्रणमाम्यहम्॥ ८॥

॥ इति श्री ब्रह्मा अष्टकम् सम्पूर्णम् ॥
transliteration:
|| Shri Brahma Ashtakam ||

Chaturvaktram Chaturbahum Srishtisthityantakaranam |
Akshamaladharam Devam Brahmanam Pranamamy Aham || 1 ||

Kamalasanam Asinam Shvetavarnam Chaturmukham |
Vedavacham Vidhataram Brahmanam Pranamamy Aham || 2 ||

Kamandaludharam Haste Sruksruvau Cha Karambuje |
Padmasane Sthitam Nityam Brahmanam Pranamamy Aham || 3 ||

Hamsavahanam Arudham Mekhalayajnasutrinam |
Hiranyagarbham Varadam Brahmanam Pranamamy Aham || 4 ||

Savitri Sahitam Devam Vedamatrisamanvitam |
Prajapatim Jagatrsrashta Brahmanam Pranamamy Aham || 5 ||

Rajasam Gunasampannam Srishtikartaram Ishvaram |
Virinchim Pitaram Vishvasya Brahmanam Pranamamy Aham || 6 ||

Chaturvedasvarupam Cha Chaturyugavidhayakam |
Adikavi Kavim Vande Brahmanam Pranamamy Aham || 7 ||

Nabhikamala Sambhutam Vishnor Narayanasya Cha |
Shatanandam Sadanandam Brahmanam Pranamamy Aham || 8 ||

|| Iti Shri Brahma Ashtakam Sampurnam ||
hindiMeaning:
चार मुख, चार भुजा, सृष्टि-स्थिति-प्रलय के कारण, अक्षमाला धारण करने वाले ब्रह्मदेव को प्रणाम।

कमलासन पर विराजमान, श्वेतवर्ण, चतुर्मुख, वेदवाणी बोलने वाले विधाता को प्रणाम।

कमण्डलु, स्रुक्-स्रुव हाथ में धारण किए, पद्मासन पर नित्य स्थित ब्रह्मा को प्रणाम।

हंसवाहन पर आरूढ़, मेखला और यज्ञसूत्र धारी, हिरण्यगर्भ, वरदायक ब्रह्मा को प्रणाम।

सावित्री सहित, वेदमाता से युक्त, प्रजापति, जगत्स्रष्टा ब्रह्मा को प्रणाम।

राजसगुणसम्पन्न, सृष्टिकर्ता, ईश्वर, विरिञ्चि, विश्व के पिता ब्रह्मा को प्रणाम।

चारों वेदों के स्वरूप, चारों युगों के विधायक, आदिकवि ब्रह्मा को प्रणाम।

विष्णु नारायण के नाभिकमल से उत्पन्न, शतानन्द, सदानन्द ब्रह्मा को प्रणाम।
benefits: Eight verses praising Lord Brahma as the Creator of the universe
Invokes Brahma's four faces representing the four Vedas
Invokes creative inspiration and intellectual clarity
Ideal for students, scholars and creative professionals
Rare devotional hymn for the worship of Prajapati Brahma
description: Brahma Ashtakam (ब्रह्मा अष्टकम्) is an eight-verse devotional hymn dedicated to Lord Brahma. Attributed to Brahma Purana, this prayer holds a special place in Hindu devotional literature. Chanting this stotra with devotion is said to grant creative inspiration and intellectual clarity as well as invoke Brahma's four faces representing the four Vedas. Additionally, it is known to rare devotional hymn for the worship of Prajapati Brahma. This prayer is traditionally recited on Thursday (Guruvar) and during Brahma Jayanti to invoke divine grace. The text contains 8 verses and takes around 4 minutes to recite, fitting conveniently into a daily spiritual routine. On this page, you can read the complete Brahma Ashtakam in Devanagari Sanskrit with English transliteration, Hindi meaning (arth), and free PDF download for offline recitation.

seoFaqs.question: What is the Brahma Ashtakam and what is its significance?
seoFaqs.answer: The Brahma Ashtakam (ब्रह्मा अष्टकम्) is an eight-verse devotional hymn glorifying Lord Brahma — the first deva of the Hindu Trimurti and the cosmic Creator (Srashta) of the universe. The composition is preserved in the Brahma Purana tradition and is among the comparatively rare stotras dedicated to Brahma — for despite his supreme position, Lord Brahma has very few temples (the most famous being the Pushkar Brahma temple in Rajasthan, established by Adi Shankaracharya). The eight verses systematically meditate on Brahma's iconographic forms — Chaturmukha (four-faced), Hamsavahana (riding the swan), Padmasana (seated on the lotus), Kamandalu-dhara (holder of the water-pot), and Sruk-sruva-dhara (holder of the yajna ladles) — each verse ending with the refrain 'Brahmanam pranamamy aham' (I bow to Brahma). (Sources: Brahma Purana; classical paddhati.)

seoFaqs.question: What do Brahma's four faces represent in the Brahma Ashtakam?
seoFaqs.answer: Brahma's chaturmukha (four faces) is one of his most distinctive iconographic features and carries deep theological meaning glorified in the Brahma Ashtakam: (1) the four faces correspond to the four Vedas — Rig (east-facing), Yajur (south), Sama (west) and Atharva (north) — Brahma is Vedavachah Vidhata, the very source of Vedic speech; (2) they represent the four yugas (Satya, Treta, Dvapara, Kali) — verse 7 calls him 'Chaturyuga-vidhayaka' (ordainer of the four yugas); (3) the four directions (digbandha) — Brahma surveys all four quarters of his creation simultaneously; (4) the four states of consciousness (jagrat, svapna, sushupti, turiya); (5) the four varnas of dharma. Originally Brahma had five faces, but Shiva severed the fifth (the upward-facing one) due to its arrogance — an event narrated in the Shiva Purana. (Sources: Brahma Purana; Shiva Purana; classical iconography.)

seoFaqs.question: What are Brahma's iconographic attributes described in the Brahma Ashtakam?
seoFaqs.answer: The Brahma Ashtakam systematically describes the complete iconographic form of the Creator: (1) Chaturvaktra and Chaturbahu — four faces and four arms; (2) Akshamala — the rudraksha-japa rosary, symbolising endless cycles of creation and time; (3) Kamandalu — the sacred water-pot, holding the cosmic waters from which creation emerged; (4) Sruk-sruva — the two yajna ladles, marking him as Yajneshvara (Lord of Sacrifice); (5) Pustaka (implied) — the sacred Vedas; (6) Padmasana — seated on the lotus that emerged from Vishnu's navel (verse 8: 'Nabhi-kamala-sambhutam Vishnor Narayanasya cha'); (7) Hamsavahana — the divine swan as his mount, symbolising viveka (the discrimination between sat and asat); (8) Mekhala and Yajnasutra — the sacred girdle and brahmanical thread; (9) Hiranyagarbha — the golden cosmic womb-form; (10) Shvetavarna — pure white complexion, representing satvika gunas. (Sources: Brahma Purana; classical Hindu iconography.)

seoFaqs.question: Why is Lord Brahma rarely worshipped despite being the Creator?
seoFaqs.answer: Several Puranic narratives explain why Brahma worship is uncommon in India: (1) the Shiva Purana relates that Brahma lied during a contest with Vishnu over who could find the ends of Shiva's infinite jyotirlinga — Brahma falsely claimed to have found the top, so Shiva cursed that Brahma would never be worshipped on earth; (2) the Skanda Purana relates that when Goddess Savitri (Brahma's first wife) found him performing a yajna at Pushkar with Gayatri (a Gujjar woman he had hurriedly married because Savitri arrived late), she cursed Brahma that he would only be worshipped at Pushkar; (3) theologically, Brahma represents the rajasic guna and the act of creation, which is complete and not requiring further phala from worship; whereas Vishnu (sthiti, palana) and Shiva (samhara, kalyana) preside over functions devotees actively need. Brahma is principally honoured at Pushkar (Rajasthan), Kumbhakonam (Tamil Nadu) and Khedbrahma (Gujarat). (Sources: Shiva Purana; Skanda Purana Pushkar Mahatmya.)

seoFaqs.question: What benefits are attributed to reciting the Brahma Ashtakam?
seoFaqs.answer: Tradition attributes the following phala to sincere recitation of the Brahma Ashtakam: (1) creative inspiration and intellectual clarity — Brahma is the Adikavi (first poet) of the universe, and his grace bestows kavi-shakti (poetic and creative power); (2) success in education, scholarship, research, writing, and Vedic studies — students recite this before exams and scholars before composing texts; (3) sharpening of viveka (discrimination) and buddhi (intellect) — symbolised by the hamsa, Brahma's swan, which traditionally separates milk from water; (4) blessings for new beginnings — laying foundation stones, starting new enterprises, beginning a new academic year, or starting any creative project; (5) progeny and family lineage as Brahma is Prajapati (Lord of progeny); (6) merit for visiting Pushkar Tirtha; (7) deepening of yajna-bhakti (sacrificial devotion). It is especially recommended for writers, teachers, scientists and creative professionals. (Sources: Brahma Purana; classical paddhati.)

seoFaqs.question: On what days and festivals should the Brahma Ashtakam be recited?
seoFaqs.answer: The most auspicious occasions for Brahma Ashtakam recitation: (1) Brahma Jayanti — Kartik Shukla Purnima (the supreme festival celebrating Brahma's manifestation, observed especially at Pushkar with the famous Pushkar Mela on the same purnima); (2) every Thursday (Guruvar) — traditionally associated with Brahma, Vishnu, and Brihaspati (the deva-guru); (3) before commencing any Vedic study (Vedarambha), Upanayana ceremony, school admission, or scholarly research; (4) at the start of any creative work — composing music, writing, painting, or scientific discovery; (5) at sunrise (since Brahma is the deity who initiated time and the day); (6) during Sarasvati Puja (Vasant Panchami / Magh Shukla Panchami) — alongside Goddess Sarasvati, Brahma's consort; (7) before laying foundation stones (bhumi pujan) for new buildings; (8) at Pushkar tirtha during Kartik snana. Daily recitation at brahma-muhurta is most potent. (Sources: Drikpanchang Brahma Jayanti calendar; classical paddhati.)
